directions.name: Restore the best surface and replace the last 64 hot rows with a dedicated 64x128 residual PTX kernel
directions.hypothesis: The recent hot-band micro-tuning attempts are mostly noise or regressions, and the latest best custom still comes from the restored pre-experiment surface. The largest untouched structural opportunity is no longer inside the 256x128 hot-band body, but in the last 64 hot rows that still fall back to the 384-wide peeled kernel. Those rows match one 64x128 warp-local PTX tile perfectly, so a dedicated residual-hot-band kernel can keep the same 64x64 warp microkernel and export path while avoiding the heavier 384 peeled path for that final strip.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual hot-row overhead from routing the last 64 rows through the generic 384 peeled kernel instead of a matching fixed-shape PTX path.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. This is a larger structural change than the recent local tweaks, but it reuses the existing hot-band PTX machinery instead of inventing a new microkernel family.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if the residual-hot-band kernel does not remove or materially reduce the peeled-kernel cost, or if correctness breaks.

directions.name: Restore-only fallback to the current best surface
directions.hypothesis: If the residual-hot-band kernel grows too large or fails validation, the safest move is to restore the current best measured surface immediately instead of staying on the padded-B branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Not a bottleneck attack; this is the restore path after a negative bank-padding experiment.
directions.risk: Low. This keeps the loop aligned with the best measured branch.
directions.stop_condition: Use this if the residual kernel does not validate quickly.

directions.name: Later: revisit export trimming only after the residual path is specialized
directions.hypothesis: If the 64-row residual path becomes another PTX hot-band variant, the export helper becomes a more central shared component across both hot regions. That would be a better time to revisit export trimming than the earlier standalone export experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared epilogue/export overhead after the residual path has been unified with the PTX hot-band family.
directions.risk: Moderate. Deferred on purpose until the larger structural change is settled.
directions.stop_condition: Do not spend a round here until the residual-hot-band path is resolved.

notes: Round 3/50 starts from another small bank-padding regression. The hot-band path remains dominant, and the recent local layout/export tweaks have not produced a durable win. Recommended direction dir_01 therefore moves to a larger but still coherent structural change: restore the current best surface and replace the final 64 hot rows with a dedicated 64x128 residual PTX kernel so those rows stop paying the 384 peeled path. Dir_02 is the restore fallback if that kernel does not validate quickly, and dir_03 defers any more export work until the residual path is specialized.
